title: Customer Due Diligence obligation
rule: Obliged entities must apply Customer Due Diligence (CDD) measures when establishing a business relationship, carrying out occasional transactions above EUR 15,000, or when there is suspicion of money laundering or terrorist financing.
details: 6AMLD Article 11. CDD includes: identifying and verifying the customer's identity, identifying the beneficial owner, assessing the purpose and intended nature of the business relationship, and conducting ongoing monitoring.
exceptions: Simplified due diligence may apply for low-risk situations (Article 15)

title: Enhanced Due Diligence for high-risk
rule: Enhanced Due Diligence (EDD) must be applied in high-risk situations including: PEPs, complex or unusually large transactions, countries identified as high-risk by the Commission, and correspondent banking relationships.
details: 6AMLD Articles 18-24. EDD measures include: obtaining additional information on the customer, obtaining senior management approval, conducting enhanced ongoing monitoring, and establishing the source of funds and source of wealth.

title: Politically Exposed Persons
rule: Obliged entities must have risk management systems to determine whether customers or beneficial owners are Politically Exposed Persons (PEPs), their family members, or known close associates.
details: 6AMLD Article 20. PEPs include: heads of state, government ministers, members of parliament, supreme court members, ambassadors, senior military officers, board members of state-owned enterprises. PEP status persists for 12 months after leaving office. Family members and close associates are also covered.
exceptions: After 12 months without public function, standard CDD may resume if risk assessment supports it

title: Suspicious Activity Reporting
rule: Obliged entities must promptly report to the Financial Intelligence Unit (FIU) when they know, suspect, or have reasonable grounds to suspect that funds are the proceeds of criminal activity or are related to terrorist financing.
details: 6AMLD Article 33. The report must be filed before the transaction is carried out (where possible). Tipping off the customer about the SAR is prohibited (Article 39). The entity and its employees are protected from liability for good-faith reporting.
exceptions: Tipping-off prohibition does not apply to attempts to dissuade a client from illegal activity

title: Beneficial ownership identification
rule: Obliged entities must identify and verify the beneficial owner(s) of legal entities. A beneficial owner is any natural person who ultimately owns or controls more than 25% of shares, voting rights, or ownership interest.
details: 6AMLD Article 3(6). If no person meets the 25% threshold, the senior managing official(s) are deemed the beneficial owner. Member states must maintain beneficial ownership registers accessible to obliged entities, FIUs, and competent authorities.
exceptions: Listed companies subject to equivalent disclosure requirements may be exempt from some BO identification requirements

title: Risk-based approach
rule: Obliged entities must assess and mitigate money laundering and terrorist financing risks through a risk-based approach. The extent of CDD measures must be commensurate with the identified risks.
details: 6AMLD Articles 6-8. The risk assessment must consider: customer risk factors, geographic risk factors, product/service/transaction risk factors, and delivery channel risk factors. The assessment must be documented, kept up to date, and made available to supervisory authorities.

title: Record retention — 5 years
rule: Obliged entities must retain copies of CDD documents and transaction records for at least 5 years after the end of the business relationship or the date of the occasional transaction.
details: 6AMLD Article 40. Records must include: documents obtained through CDD measures, supporting evidence and records of transactions (sufficient to permit reconstruction of individual transactions), and correspondence.
exceptions: Member states may permit or require retention for an additional period of up to 5 years (10 years total) after a thorough assessment

title: EUR 15,000 occasional transaction threshold
rule: CDD must be applied for occasional transactions amounting to EUR 15,000 or more, whether carried out in a single operation or in several operations that appear to be linked.
details: 6AMLD Article 11(b). For currency exchange and money transmission, the threshold is EUR 1,000. For gambling, the threshold is EUR 2,000. Obliged entities must be vigilant about structured transactions designed to avoid thresholds.
exceptions: Lower thresholds for specific sectors: EUR 1,000 for money transmission, EUR 2,000 for gambling, EUR 10,000 for traders in goods (cash payments)

title: Virtual asset service provider obligations
rule: Virtual Asset Service Providers (VASPs) — including crypto exchanges and custodial wallet providers — are obliged entities subject to the full AML/KYC framework.
details: 6AMLD Article 2(1)(3)(g). VASPs must: register or obtain authorization, apply CDD to all customers, monitor transactions, file SARs, comply with the travel rule (originator and beneficiary information for transfers).

title: Sanctions screening obligation
rule: Obliged entities must screen customers, beneficial owners, and transactions against EU sanctions lists and ensure no business relationships are maintained with sanctioned persons or entities.
details: Council Regulation (EC) No 881/2002 and subsequent regulations. Screening must be performed at onboarding, periodically during the relationship, and when lists are updated. Matches must be reported to the national competent authority and assets frozen immediately.
exceptions: Humanitarian exemptions may apply in specific cases with prior authorization from competent authorities
source: Council Regulation (EC) No 881/2002; EU sanctions framework

title: Ongoing monitoring obligation
rule: Obliged entities must conduct ongoing monitoring of the business relationship, including scrutiny of transactions to ensure they are consistent with the entity's knowledge of the customer, their business and risk profile.
details: 6AMLD Article 11(d). Ongoing monitoring includes: ensuring documents and data are kept up to date, reviewing source of funds for high-risk relationships, and detecting complex or unusual transaction patterns.

title: Employee training requirement
rule: Obliged entities must ensure that employees who are involved in AML/CFT compliance receive appropriate training on recognizing and handling suspicious transactions.
details: 6AMLD Article 46. Training must cover: applicable legislation and regulations, recognizing suspicious transactions, CDD procedures, SAR filing procedures, data protection obligations. Training must be regular and documented.

title: Criminal liability for money laundering
rule: Money laundering is a criminal offence punishable by imprisonment. The 6th AMLD harmonizes the definition across EU member states and sets a maximum penalty of at least 4 years imprisonment.
details: Directive (EU) 2018/1673 (6AMLD on criminal law). Predicate offences include: participation in an organised criminal group, terrorism, trafficking, fraud, corruption, tax crimes, and environmental crime. Self-laundering (laundering proceeds of crimes you committed) is also criminalised.

title: Identity verification methods
rule: Customer identity must be verified using reliable and independent sources. For natural persons: government-issued ID document. For legal entities: register extract or certificate of incorporation.
details: 6AMLD Article 13. Verification may use: documents (passport, national ID card), electronic identification (eID, eIDAS), video identification (where permitted by member state), or qualified trust services. Remote onboarding (non-face-to-face) requires additional measures.
exceptions: Simplified CDD for low-risk situations may allow reduced verification (Article 15)

title: High-risk third countries
rule: The European Commission identifies third countries with strategic deficiencies in their AML/CFT regimes. Enhanced Due Diligence is mandatory for business relationships and transactions involving these countries.
details: 6AMLD Article 18(1). The list is based on FATF assessments. EDD measures for high-risk countries include: obtaining additional information on the customer and beneficial owner, obtaining additional information on the intended nature of the business relationship, and enhanced monitoring.

title: Simplified Due Diligence conditions
rule: Simplified CDD may be applied where lower risk is identified through an adequate risk assessment. Simplified measures must still allow detection of unusual or suspicious transactions.
details: 6AMLD Article 15. Lower risk factors include: publicly listed companies, public administrations, customers in jurisdictions with effective AML/CFT systems, products with limited value or functionality. Simplified CDD is NOT exemption from CDD — it permits reduced scope.
exceptions: Simplified CDD must NOT be applied where there is suspicion of ML/TF, regardless of other risk factors

title: Internal reporting procedures
rule: Obliged entities must establish internal procedures for employees to report suspicious activities to the MLRO/compliance officer, who then decides whether to file a SAR with the FIU.
details: 6AMLD Article 46(1). Procedures must include: clear escalation paths, protection for whistleblowers, documentation requirements, and timelines. The nominated officer (MLRO) must have sufficient authority and resources to act independently.

title: FATF 40 Recommendations
rule: The Financial Action Task Force (FATF) 40 Recommendations are the international standard for AML/CFT measures. EU AML Directives implement these recommendations into EU law.
details: FATF Recommendations cover: AML/CFT policies, money laundering offence, terrorist financing offence, confiscation, CDD and record-keeping, reporting suspicious transactions, regulation and supervision, transparency of legal persons, international cooperation. Countries are assessed through Mutual Evaluation Reports.

title: Travel Rule for crypto transfers
rule: For transfers of virtual assets, VASPs must obtain and transmit originator and beneficiary information: name, account number (wallet address), and address/national ID/customer number/date of birth.
details: Regulation (EU) 2023/1113 (Transfer of Funds Regulation recast). Applies to all crypto transfers regardless of amount (unlike traditional wire transfers which have a EUR 1,000 threshold for some simplified measures). Both ordering and beneficiary VASPs must comply.
exceptions: Self-hosted wallet transfers: the VASP must still collect beneficiary information but transmission depends on the transfer amount and risk assessment

title: Customer risk rating
rule: Each customer must be assigned a risk rating (low, medium, high) based on a documented risk assessment considering customer type, geography, products/services, transaction patterns, and delivery channels.
details: Risk scoring should be dynamic and updated based on ongoing monitoring. Common risk factors: PEP status, high-risk country nexus, complex ownership structures, cash-intensive business, new products/technologies, non-face-to-face relationships, third-party reliance.
source: Directive (EU) 2018/843, Article 8; EBA Guidelines on ML/TF risk factors
